observations.recommendation:
1.17. We reiterated our recommendations and Management agreed to instruct
the Municipal Accountant to (a) record the appropriate amount of impairment
loss on overdue Accounts Receivable in accordance with paragraph 87 of IPSAS
No. 41; (b) employ all appropriate remedies to recover overdue receivables,
including issuance of demand letters and initiating legal proceedings, if necessary;
and (c) request for an authority from COA to write-off dormant receivables
following the requirements stated under COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated
August 17, 2023.

2.15. We recommended and Management agreed to undertake the following
courses of action: (a) the Municipal Accountant to set up the RPT/SET
Receivables at the beginning of the year based on the Certified List of Taxpayers
and Tax Dues prepared by the Office of the Municipal Treasurer; and (b)
Municipal Treasurer to improve collection strategies on RPT/SET and initiate the
available remedial measures in collecting the overdue taxes from delinquent
taxpayers.

3.9. We recommended and Management agreed to direct: (a) the GSO/Supply
Division to maintain Stock/Property Cards for each commodity/stock to comply
with the required maintenance of complete and updated inventory records and (b)
the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs)
for each commodity/stock to provide competent evidence to document the
accuracy of the cost of inventories.

4.28. We recommended that Management instruct the: (a) Inventory
Committee and Property Officer to (i) prioritize the conduct of complete
physical inventory of the Municipality’s PPE in accordance with the guidelines
provided under paragraphs 6.1 and 6.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006; (ii)
render an accurate and complete Report on the Physical Count of PPE
(RPCPPE) as basis of the Accounting Unit to correct the reported PPE balances
in the Financial Statements; and (iii) prepare an Inventory and Inspection
Report on Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) for PPE items identified as
unserviceable/beyond economic repair and due for disposal; (b) Accounting
Office and GSO to (i) undertake collaborative efforts to reconcile the RCPPE
with the books of accounts following the guidelines provided under paragraph
6.3 of the aforementioned COA Circular No. 2020-006; (ii) proceed with the
disposition and/or derecognition of the non-existing/missing items of PPE
guided by paragraphs 7.0 and 8.0 of the same COA Circular; and (iii)
subsequently conduct reconciliation on a regular basis to maintain the accuracy
of reported items of PPE; and (c) Municipal Accountant to compute the
depreciation expense for each item of PPE pursuant to Section 4, Chapter 2 of
NGAS Manual for LGUs, Vol. I and Item 4.0 of COA Circular No. 2003-007.

5.5. We recommended and Management agreed to maintain its compliance
with the guidelines outlined in Section 50 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume
I, to ensure the fair presentation of the balances in the affected accounts within
the financial statements.

6.9. We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that the Acting
Municipal Accountant: (a) verify, analyze and validate the existence of dormant
accounts, and effect proper disposition of said accounts in accordance with
prescribed procedures; and (b) leverage on the one-time cleansing of all dormant
receivable accounts as provided by COA Circular No. 2023-008.

7.13. We recommended and Management agreed to: a) continue its adherence to
Section 287 of RA No. 7160, as well as DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum
Circular (JMC) No. 1, dated November 4, 2020, regarding the appropriation and
utilization of the 20% Development Fund; and b) abide strictly with the
prioritization of projects embodied in the 5-year comprehensive development plan
in compliance with item 3.2.1 of the same JMC, or require revising the CDP.

8.21. We recommended that Management ensure, through the (a) BPLO: to (i)
strictly enforce the submission of Audited Financial Statements, or in their
absence, require a Sworn Statement of Gross Receipts/Sales or a copy of the ITR
from taxpayers, to ensure the proper computation of any deficiency tax; (ii)
exercise greater diligence in the implementation and enforcement of the provisions
of the Municipal Revenue Code; (iii) ensure that the applicable tax rates are
correctly applied based on the specific line of business declared by each taxpayer;
(b) Municipal Treasurer to examine and verify the books of accounts and other
pertinent records of any business establishments; and (c) BPLO and Municipal
Treasurer to verify, assess, and validate other businesses aside from the three
noted businesses and demand the payment of computed under collection of
business taxes.

9.27. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the (a)
Management to (i) continue and further strengthen their efforts in ensuring
substantial compliance with the guidelines governing the appropriation,
utilization, and accounting of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
Fund (LDRRMF), in accordance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10121,
DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 dated March 25, 2013, and
COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and (ii) prioritize the
immediate appropriation of funds for filling the mandatory plantilla positions in
the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRO), as
required under RA No. 10121; (b) the MDRRMO to: (i) diligently monitor and
ensure the efficient utilization of the LDRRM Funds, in accordance with the
objectives outlined in the LDRRM Plans; and (ii) periodically reassess the
feasibility of the PPAs to identify any unimplementable or duplicative activities;
and (c) the Acting Municipal Accountant to facilitate the transfer of the Special
Trust Fund that has lapsed its statutory period to the General Fund, in compliance
with Section 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

10.8. We recommended and Management agreed to continue implementing
these proven and effective methods to sustain its pursuit of gender equality and
increased awareness of gender issues and women's empowerment.

11.9. We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Human
Resources Management Officer (MHRMO) to: (i) prioritize the filling of vacant
regular positions; (ii) continue to adhere to the requirements prescribed on hiring
and payment of wages for JO personnel; and, (ii) facilitate coordination with
Department Heads to determine the actual number of JOs needed to augment the
existing workforce in each office/unit, thereby maximizing the services of
permanent employees.

12.8. We recommended and Management agreed to direct the: (a) Municipal
HRMO, to ensure the continued updating of the leave records of the
Municipality’s employees to maintain accuracy and compliance with COA
Circular No. 2024-009, dated July 16, 2024; and (b) Municipal Accountant to
update the provisions for earned leave credits on a quarterly basis in accordance
with the recognition and measurement principles outlined in the Circular.

13.16. We recommended and Management agreed to instruct: (a) the Municipal
Accountant to ensure the submission to the Audit Team of the paid DVs and
supporting documents, and Trial Balances within the prescribed period; and (b)
the Municipal Budget Officer to promptly submit the monthly SAOBs to the
Audit Team.

14.4. We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal
Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to (a) continue the practice of complying
with the proper withholding of taxes; and (b) strictly observe the required full and
intact remittance of all withheld amount to avoid or minimize the accumulation of
unremitted balance in the custody of the agency, consistent with Section 114 (c) of
the National Revenue Code as amended by RA No. 9337 and Revenue Regulation
No. 2-98.

15.5. We recommended and Management agreed to continue complying with the
provisions of RA No. 8291 and its IRR, particularly on the prompt remittance to the
GSIS of the employees’ premium contributions and loan amortizations, as well as the
government share for each month.

16.4. We commended the Management for its religious compliance with the Home
Development Mutual Fund Law of 2009 on the proper withholding of premiums and
loan amortizations of its employees and the prompt remittance of the same, together
with the Municipality’s share to the HDMF. We also recommended, and
Management agreed, that this compliance be continuously practiced by the
Municipality.

17.5. We recommended and Management agreed to continue to adhere to the
National Health Insurance Act of 2013 (RA No. 7875 as amended by RA Nos. 9241
and 10606) and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations to ensure the
ongoing health insurance coverage of its employees.

18.7. We recommended and Management agreed to: (a) continuously observe
the accounting and auditing rules and regulations, particularly in ensuring the
regularity, validity, completeness of transactions’ documents among others, to
prevent the incurrence of similar deficiencies which resulted in the issuance of
audit suspension; (b) cause the immediate submission of lacking documents
identified in the remaining unsettled Notices of Suspensions issued by the Audit
Team; and (c) demand the person/s named liable in the Notice of Disallowances
for the immediate settlement of their respective accountabilities.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct: (a) the GSO/Supply Division to maintain Stock/Property Cards for each commodity/stock to comply with the required maintenance of complete and updated inventory records and (b) the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain Supplies Ledger Cards (SLCs) for each commodity/stock to provide competent evidence to document the accuracy of the cost of inventories.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the LCE:
(a) institute legal remedies to enforce the settlement of cash advances granted to officials and employees, as applicable; and enforcing the liquidation of
(b) direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to initiate procedures for the application of the request for write-off if the conditions and circumstances prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005 on writing off the accounts would be present.
We also recommended that:
(a) Management refrain from granting additional cash advances to officers and employees with unsettled outstanding cash advances during the year; and
(b) LCE issue a memorandum or designation for officer who shall act as Special Disbursing Officers.
We likewise recommended that the Management strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 89 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting, liquidation and utilization of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The Acting Municipal Accountant has already started the implementation of outstanding cash advances during the year

observations.recommendation:
In view of the above audit observations, we reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
(a) Municipal Treasurer to:
(i) prepare a complete Certified List of Taxpayers, bearing the names, amount due and collectibles during the year, and
(ii) submit the same to the accounting unit as the basis in setting up of RPT/SEF tax receivables in the succeeding year; and
(b) Accountant to set-up the RPT/SEF tax receivables at the beginning of the year based on the Certified List of Taxpayers and Tax Due prepared by the Office of the Municipal Treasurer.
Further, we recommend that the LCE require the:
(a) Municipal Treasurer to update and properly filled out the RPTAR particularly the data on the tax dues and the balance of unpaid taxes; and
(b) Treasurer and Accountant to regularly conduct reconciliation of their records.
Moreover, we recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Treasurer to improve collection strategies on RPT and initiate the available remedial measures in collecting the overdue taxes from delinquent taxpayers.

In view of the above audit observations, we recommended that:
(a) Management:
(i) continue to comply with Section 287 of the RA 7160 and DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No.1 dated November 4, 2020 on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% DF;
(ii) ensure proper planning and monitoring of the implementation of the PPAs in accordance with the agreed timelines; and
(iii) submit the required documents in order to justify the delay in the project completion of two projects discussed above; and
(b) the Executive and Legislative offices work closely on the timely passage of Annual Investment Plan and Executive Budget as required in Section 319 of the Local Government Code.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
(a) closely monitor and ensure optimum utilization of the LDRRM Funds in accordance with the LDRRM Plan; and
(b) cause the immediate creation and filling up of the required personnel and constitute an Emergency Response Team (ERT) as required under RA No. 10121 of the Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction Management Act of 2010.

For the aforesaid deficiencies, we reiterated the previous year’s recommendations that the LCE:
(a) issue an Executive Order installing the required structures and composition of GFPS; and
(b) direct the GFPS to:
(i) continue to comply with the allocation of the prescribed budget for GAD and the preparation of a relevant GAD Budget and Plan pursuant to the pertinent laws and regulations on GAD;
(ii) monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs in accordance with GAD Plans and Budget as reviewed by the DILG; and
(iii)timely submit to the Audit Team the GAD Accomplishment Report in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-001.
Moreover, we recommended that the Executive Office and the Sangguniang Bayan prioritize the passage of Gender and Development Code to address the current gender gaps within the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the:
(a) Acting Human Resources and Management Officer to:
(i) immediately submit the lacking documents, as discussed above in proper form to the Office of the Auditor in order to render the appropriate audit action thereon;
(ii) submit regularly to the Audit Team the copies of Contracts for Job Order (JO) and Contract of Service (COS) workers within the prescribed period as required by COA Circular No. 2009-001; and
(iii) timely assess the agency’s functions, systems and procedures, organizational structure, and staffing pattern to figure out the needs and gaps and to determine the required personnel complement periodically; and
(b) Acting Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of supporting documents to substantiate the payroll claims.
We likewise recommended that the LCE, Vice Mayor and SB Members justify the necessity and reasonableness of procuring a relatively excessive number of JOs/COs assigned to their respective offices.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
(a) instruct the Municipal Treasurer and the OIC Municipal Accountant to continue the practice of complying with the withholding of taxes; and
(b) direct the aforesaid officials to strictly observe the required full and intact remittance of all withheld amount to avoid or minimize the accumulation of this unremitted balance in the custody of the agency, consistent with Section 114 (c) of the National Revenue Code as amended by RA No. 9337 and Revenue Regulation No. 2-98.

We recommended that Management continue to perform the agency’s mandate as withholding agent of the government with emphasis on strict observance of regulation on timely and intact remittances of employees’ and employers’ contributions in accordance with Section 6 (b) of Republic Act No. 8291 dated May 30, 1997.

We recommended that Management direct the OIC Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to:
(a) maintain observance of the accounting and auditing rules and regulations, particularly in ensuring the regularity, validity, completeness of documentation of the transactions, among others, to prevent the incurrence of similar deficiencies which resulted in the issuance of audit suspension;
(b) facilitate the immediate submission of lacking documents identified in the respective Notices of Suspensions issued by the Audit Team; and
(c) prepare/issue demand letters to the persons named liable in the Notice of Disallowances for the settlement of their respective accountabilities.
Further, in the event that subsequent issuance of audit suspension occurs, immediate compliance by Management in the enforcement of settlement is advised to avoid maturing of the same into disallowance.

1.21. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Acting
Municipal Accountant to: (a) issue demand letters to all the
accountable officials with outstanding receivables particularly those that aged more
than ten years, and exhaust all means to collect dormant receivable accounts; (b)
regularly verify, analyze and validate the existence of the receivables and unliquidated
cash advances, and effect proper disposition of said accounts in accordance with
prescribed procedures; and (c) leverage on the one-time cleansing of all dormant
receivable accounts as provided by COA Circular No. 2023-008.
1.22. During the exit conference conducted on May 17, 2024, Management agreed
with the audit recommendations. The Acting Municipal Accountant also committed to
exert best effort to gather the documents and follow the necessary procedures within
the third and fourth quarters of CY 2024.

2.30. In view of the above audit observations, we reiterated our previous years’
recommendations that the Local Chief Executive direct the: (a) Municipal
Treasurer to: (i) prepare a complete Certified List of Taxpayers, bearing the
names, amount due and collectibles during the year, and submit the same to the
accounting unit as the latter’s basis in setting up of RPT/SET Receivables for the
succeeding year; and (b) Accountant to set-up the RPT/SET Receivables at the
beginning of the year based on the Certified List of Taxpayers and Tax Due
prepared by the Office of the Municipal Treasurer. Further, we recommended
that the LCE require the (a) Municipal Treasurer to update and properly fill out
the RPTAR particularly the data on tax dues and the balance of unpaid taxes;
(b) adhere to the income target computation prescribed in BLGF issuances;
(c) improve collection strategies on RPT and initiate the available remedial
measures in collecting the overdue taxes from delinquent taxpayers; and (d)
Treasurer and Accountant to regularly conduct reconciliation of their respective
records.
2.31. During the exit conference, the Acting Municipal Treasurer presented and
submitted a complete summarized list of Certified List of Taxpayers, for Audit Team’s
verification.
2.32. Management agreed with the audit recommendations and committed to start the
implementation within the third quarter of CY 2023.

3.56. In relation to the above observations, we recommended that the LCE
instruct the: (1) Municipal Accountant to (a) prepare the necessary journal entry
to cause the immediate correction of the erroneous dropping-off of PPE accounts;
(b) rectify the capitalized cost of repairs and allocate them to the appropriate
expense account; (c) record the donated machineries; and (d) compute the
depreciation expense for each item of PPE pursuant to Section 4, Chapter 2 of
NGAS Manual for LGUs, Vol. I and Item 4.0 of COA Circular No. 2003-007;
(2) Municipal Accountant and Property Officer to (a) maintain their respective
PPE records and reconcile their records at least on a quarterly basis; (b) conduct
a complete physical count of all PPE items and submit the RPCPPE to the Auditor
pursuant to Section 124 of COA Circular No. 2002-003; (c) complete the necessary
procedures enumerated in COA Circular No. 2020-006 on the missing/unlocated
PPE items; and (d) consider the guidelines on one-time cleansing of PPE accounts
to render a reliable PPE information; and (3) Disposal Committee to facilitate the
disposal of unserviceable PPE in accordance with the prescribed guidelines and
procedures.

4.21. In view of the aforesaid audit observations, we recommended the
Management to: (a) promptly evaluate the existing Local Revenue Code to address
the inconsistencies therein; (b) require the Executive and Legislative Offices to
proactively work hand in hand to pass an updated Local Revenue Code; and
(c) ensure that all taxes, fees, and charges imposed by the LGU have legal basis.
4.22. During the exit conference, Management informed the Audit Team that the
Revenue and Market Codes were already drafted and passed to the Sangguniang Bayan
for approval. They further agreed to implement the other audit recommendations in the
remaining period of CY 2024.

5.37. In view of the above audit observations, we reiterated that Management:
(a) re-evaluate the policies on selection of beneficiaries for Local Social Pension;
(b) stop payment of Local Social Pension to employed Senior Citizens; (c)
formulate policy on the grant of AICS and other financial assistances that would
define, among others the: (i) qualifications/eligibilities of the beneficiaries; (ii)
commensurate amount of grant for designated condition and/or situation; (iii)
procedures to be employed; and (iv) grievance mechanism and monitoring; and
(d) implement regular and comprehensive program monitoring to ensure effective
distribution and utilization of financial assistance.
5.38. During the exit conference, Management agreed with the audit
recommendations and committed to implement the same within the remaining months
of CY 2024.

6.27. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the: (a) Acting
Municipal Accountant and Budget Officer to: (i) perform their duties and
functions as provided under the Manual on NGAS for LGUs; and (ii) install
effective control mechanisms to ensure that all disbursements do not exceed the
budgeted amounts in compliance with Section 4.1 of PD No. 1445; and (b)
members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to: (i) adhere to the above
provisions on the general policy on competitive bidding and prohibition of
splitting of contracts; and (ii) resort only to alternative modes of procurement if
the conditions are met as provided in the Revised IRR of RA 9184.
6.28. During the exit conference, Management agreed with the audit
recommendations.

7.19. We recommended and the Management agreed to: (a) require the
concerned personnel to prepare and submit regularly the Monthly Report of
Official Travels and Report of Fuel Consumption of Government Motor vehicles;
(b) ensure that all supporting documents are properly accomplished and
transmitted for audit; and (c) establish and implement effective control/process
over the use of government vehicles and fuel consumption as provided in COA
Circular No. 77-61.

8.17. We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
(a) require the members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), including the
BAC Secretariat, to: (i) adhere to the provisions on the general policy in
competitive bidding and prohibition of splitting of contracts; and (ii) resort only
to alternative modes of procurement if the conditions are met as provided in the
Revised IRR of RA 9184; (b) implement a comprehensive and structured planning
process ensuring that projects are specifically identified in both the Annual
Investment Plan (AIP) and the Annual Procurement Plan (APP), and; (c) ensure
that all disbursements are completely documented as required in Section 4 (6) of
PD No. 1445 and Appendix A, Annex H of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

9.25. We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive
instruct the Acting HRMO to: (a) formulate clear and specific standard/guidelines
for hiring/rehiring personnel on job order contract; (b) coordinate with the
Department Heads to assess and review the necessity of hiring JOs in order to
maximize the services of permanent employees and at the same time minimize
expenses on salaries/wages of JO personnel.

10.37. In view of the above audit observations, we recommended and
Management agreed to: (a) continuously adhere with the guidelines set by Section
287 of R.A. No. 7160 or the Local Government Code of 1991 and DBM-DOF-DILG
JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020 on the appropriation for Development Fund;
(b) ensure proper planning and monitoring of the implementation of the PPAs; (c)
ensure that the Comprehensive Development Plan will be updated consistent with
item 3.2.1 of the DBM-DOF-DILG- JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020; (d)
submit the required documents including liquidation reports with supporting
documents after implementation of the developmental projects and return any
unutilized fund balances; (d) instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the
necessary adjusting entry for the improper recognition of the funds transferred to
the two barangays by debiting Due from LGUs and crediting Prior Period
Adjustment in the amount of ₱4,000,000.00.

11.27. Based on the aforesaid audit observations, we recommended that
Management: (a) conduct a thorough review of existing protocols and procedures
and identify areas of inefficiency or mismanagement; (b) implement targeted
training programs for personnel involved in fund utilization to ensure compliance
with guidelines and maximize the fund's effectiveness in disaster risk reduction
initiatives; (c) closely monitor and ensure optimum utilization of the LDRRM
Funds in accordance with the LDRRM Plans; and (d) cause the immediate
appropriation of funds in order to fill up of the mandatory plantilla positions in the
MDRRO as required under RA No. 10121.
11.28. Moreover, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the
Acting Municipal Accountant to: (a) prepare the necessary adjusting journal
entries to correct the balances of the STF – LDDRMF; and (b) ensure that the
guidelines enumerated under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12,
2012 on the accounting and reporting of LDRRMF are fully complied with.
11.29. During the exit conference, the Acting Municipal Accountant disclosed that the
STF – LDRRMF was already adjusted as of April 30, 2024.
11.30. Management agreed with the audit recommendations and committed to ensure
its compliance with pertinent rules and regulations.

12.22. In view of the above audit observations, we recommended and
Management agreed to: (a) continue to adhere with the provisions of RA No. 9003
or the SWM Act of 2000 on the appropriation and implementation of its SWM
Program; (b) rigorously implement the Municipal Ordinances in relation to SWM
and impose sanctions to violators, if warranted; and (c) take appropriate action to
expedite the request for assistance from the donor agency on the use of donated
machineries to make them operational.

13.20. In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that Management:
(a) continue its good performance on the timely implementation of projects and
prompt liquidation of fund transfers received from National Government
Agencies; (b) adhere strictly on the timely submission of required documents and
financial reports to facilitate a more thorough and effective audit process; and
(c) cause the immediate submission of lacking documents identified pursuant to
Section 9 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
13.21. On January 31, 2024, the Acting Municipal Engineer submitted the lacking
documents to the Audit Team.
13.22. During the exit conference, Management agreed with the audit
recommendations and committed to (a) conduct regular monitoring in the
implementation of various projects funded by the National Government Agencies; and
(b) be more prudent in the submission of documents within the reglementary period.

14.32. We recommended that Management: (a) strictly comply with the allocation
of the prescribed budget for GAD pursuant to Republic Act No. 9710 or the Magna
Carta of Women; (b) instruct the GFPS to monitor the full implementation of
GAD PPAs in accordance with GAD Plan and Budget as reviewed by the DILG;
(c) comply with the required structure and composition of the GFPS; and
(d) instruct the GFPS to create a centralized GAD database that would consolidate
the information gathered and compiled by all offices to include gender statistics
and other relevant sex-disaggregated data, as basis in GAD planning,
programming and policy making.
14.33. We further recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan give priority for the
approval of the GAD Code to provide updated guidelines to effectively address
existing gender disparities within the Municipality and ensure alignment with R.A.
No. 9710 or The Magna Carta of Women.
14.34. During the exit conference, the Municipal Administrator informed the Audit
Team that on April 4, 2024, an Executive Order was issued to amend the composition
of the GFPS to comply with the composition required by the guidelines.
14.35. Management agreed with the recommendations and committed to start the
implementation during CY 2024.

15.31. In view of the above audit observations, we recommended and
Management agreed to: (a) improve planning and resource allocation of the SEF
through: (i) conducting a thorough review of its planning processes to identify
gaps and inefficiencies contributing to the low utilization rate of the SEF; and
(ii) implement measures to enhance planning mechanisms to ensure better
allocation and management of SEF resources, with primary focus on addressing
the educational needs of students in Aliaga; (b) discontinue the allocation of funds
for expenses that are not among those prioritized to be charged against the SEF;
(c) promptly formulate a comprehensive three-year program in accordance with
DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017; and (d) ensure that the
three-year program aligns with the priorities and objectives outlined in the
Circular, particularly in financing priority PPAs aimed at supporting the
education sector.

16.3. We recommended and Management agreed to strictly comply with Section
114 (c) of the National Revenue Code as amended by RA No. 9337 and Revenue
Regulation No. 2-98 requiring the remittance of all taxes withheld within 10th day
of the succeeding month, supporting the national government to raise more
revenues to finance government operations.

17.5. We recommended and Management agreed to continue perform the
agency’s mandate as withholding agent of the government with emphasis on strict
observance of regulation on timely and intact remittances of employees’ and
employers’ contributions in accordance with Section 6 (b) of Republic Act No.
8291 dated May 30, 1997.

18.3. We recommended and Management agreed to continuously observe the
practice of remitting Pag-IBIG contributions/premiums on a timely basis so that
the employees can enjoy all the privileges of a Pag-IBIG member.

19.5. We recommended and Management agreed to strictly observe the practice
of intact and timely remittance of employees’ and employer’s contributions due to
PhilHealth in accordance with Section 18 of the IRR of the National Health
Insurance Act of 2013 and PhilHealth Circular No. 1, s. 2014.

20.7. We recommended and Management agreed to: direct the Acting Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to: (a) maintain observance of the
accounting and auditing rules and regulations, particularly in ensuring the
regularity, validity, completeness of documentation of the transactions, among
others, to prevent the incurrence of similar deficiencies which resulted in the
issuance of audit suspension; (b) facilitate the immediate submission of lacking
documents identified in the respective Notices of Suspensions issued by the Audit
Team; and (c) prepare/issue demand letters to the persons named liable in the
Notice of Disallowances for the settlement of their respective accountabilities.
Further, in the event that subsequent issuance of audit suspension occurs,
immediate compliance by Management in the enforcement of settlement is advised
to avoid maturing of the same into disallowance.

In view of the above audit observations, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the:

(b) Acting Municipal Accountant to strengthen her review function on the requisite documentation of procurements.
We also recommended that the LCE direct the BAC Secretariat to immediately submit the lacking bidding documents of the winning bidders for the three infrastructure projects; otherwise, provide satisfactory explanation of the non-submission thereof.

In relation to the aforesaid audit observations, we recommended the Management to:
(1) submit justification for the payment of the OCA using general funds of the Municipality; and
(2) ensure that all disbursements are supported with an appropriation law or other specific statutory authority in compliance with Section 4.1 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The Audit Team issued a Notice of Suspension (NS) dated December 18, 2023 requiring the submission of legal basis and/or justification for the said disbursement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management strictly observe the practice of intact and timely remittances of employees’ and employers’ contributions due to PhilHealth in accordance with Section 18 of the IRR of the National Health Insurance Act of 2013 and PhilHealth Circular No. 1, s. 2014.

We recommended that Management be more diligent in performing the agency’s mandate as withholding agent of the government with emphasis on strict observance of Pag-IBIG regulation on intact and timely remittances of employees’ and employers’ contributions in accordance with Section 3 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9679 or the Home Development Mutual Fund Law of 2009 otherwise known as Pag-IBIG Fund.

In connection to the foregoing, we recommended that the Executive and Legislative offices of the Municipality of Aliaga: (a) exert their best effort and work together on the eventual passage of Annual Investment Plan and Executive Budget; and (b) perform their duties and functions as mandated by RA No. 7160 or the Local Government Code of 1991 in order to deliver the basic services needed by the people of Aliaga.

In view of the aforementioned, we recommended the management to justify the amount spent in excess of the CY 2018 budget and desist from applying the CY 2019 Annual Budget as basis of incurring obligation. We further recommended that management henceforth comply with Section 323 of R.A. No. 7160 and Section 4 of P.D. No. 1445 as regards to budget appropriation and disbursements.

In view of the above observations, we recommended the management to desist from applying the CY 2019 Annual Budget as basis of incurring obligation for Personnel Services (PS). We further recommended that management henceforth comply with Section 323 of R.A. No. 7160 and Section 4 of P.D. No. 1445 as regards to budget appropriation and disbursements.

We recommended and the management agreed to: (a) adopt appropriate and legal remedies to enforce the settlement of cash advances granted to officials and employees, as applicable; (b) file a request for authority to write off all dormant unliquidated cash advances to the COA Audit Team Leader and/or Supervising Auditor pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016, if warranted; and (c) continue to strictly observe the provisions of Section 89 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the granting, liquidation and utilization of cash advances.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the: (a) Municipal Treasurer to (i) prepare a complete Certified List of Taxpayers, bearing the names, amount due and collectibles during the year, and submit the same to the accounting unit as the basis in setting up of RPT/SEF tax receivables in the succeeding year; and (b) Accountant to set-up the RPT/SEF tax receivables at the beginning of the year based on the Certified List of Taxpayers and Tax Due prepared by the Office of the Municipal Treasurer.  Further, we recommended that the LCE require the (a) Municipal Treasurer to update and properly filled out the RPTAR particularly the data on the tax dues and the balance of unpaid taxes; and (b) Treasurer and Accountant to regularly conduct reconciliation of their records of the two offices.
Moreover, we recommended and the management agreed to improve collection strategies on RPT and initiate the available remedial measures in collecting the overdue taxes from delinquent taxpayers.

We recommended and the management agreed to: (a) issue demand letters to all the accountable officials with outstanding cash advances particularly those that are aged more than ten years, and exhaust all legal means to collect dormant receivable accounts; (b) regularly verify, analyze and validate the existence of the receivables and unliquidated cash advances, and effect proper disposition of said accounts in accordance with prescribed procedures; and (c) provide for allowance for impairment for dormant receivables and cash advance accounts in accordance with PPSAS 29.
We also recommended and the management agreed to request from COA for authority to write off the identified dormant accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

For the deficiencies discussed above, we reiterated and the management agreed: (a) that the Municipal Accountant and the GSO exert more efforts in locating documents pertinent to the acquisition of its PPEs, maintain their respective PPE records, and thereafter, conduct at least on a quarterly basis, the reconciliation of their PPE information; (b) that the Municipal Assessor to facilitate the registration and obtain titles for all real properties over which the Municipality has interest and ownership; and (c) to consider the one-time cleaning of PPE account balances to improve the reliability of the PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang Bayan place the general welfare of the people as top most priority in performing their respective roles and functions; and henceforth, extend cooperation, coordinate, and facilitate the timely enactment of the budget ordinance to include the Annual Investment Plan for local development.

We recommended that the Management: (a) continue its efforts to substantially comply with the guidelines set forth by R.A. No. 10121 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 2013-1; (b) discontinue the use of CY 2019 Annual Budget as basis for the next year’s LDRRMF utilization until budget and AIP were authorized.
We also recommended that the Legislative and Executive offices should work together on satisfying the requirements of the National Government so that the grants and aids eventually pass to the hands of the expecting beneficiaries of the Municipality.

For the aforesaid deficiencies, we recommended and the management agreed to: (a) issue an Executive Order installing the required structures and composition of GFPS; and (b) direct the GFPS to: (i) continue to comply with the allocation of the prescribed budget for GAD and the preparation of a relevant GAD Budget and Plan pursuant to the pertinent laws and regulations on GAD; (ii) monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs in accordance with GAD Plans and Budget as reviewed by the DILG; and (iii) timely submit to the Audit Team the GAD Accomplishment Report in compliance with COA Circular No. 2014-001.
Moreover, we recommended that the Executive Office and the Sangguniang Bayan prioritize the Gender and Development Code to address the current gender gaps within the Municipality and to align the code with R.A. No. 9710 or the Magna Carta for Women.

We commended the Management for its continuous efforts to comply with the COVID-19 vaccination program guidelines, a way of providing protection to their constituents from adverse effects of the virus.
We likewise recommended that the: (a) Local Chief Executive continue to intensify the information campaign to the public to spread awareness of COVID-19 vaccine benefits to achieve the Herd Immunity in the Municipality; and (b) the Municipal Officials to coordinate with each other as regards on actions of the Vaccination Program in order to establish, monitor and report the vaccination statistics of the Municipality.

We reiterated the previous year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to (i) competently perform the mandate of her Office; (ii) prudently conduct her duties and functions in accordance with applicable laws, rules and regulations; (ii) lead the management on developing and exercising decisions as regards budget implementation and accountability; and (iv) complement the financial information of the Municipality by maintaining reliable registries, records and reports on budgetary appropriation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

(c) Instruct the Local DRRM Officer to (i) develop Contingency Plans/Standard Operating Procedure for other hazards including but not limited to earthquake and vehicular accident; (ii) provide a hotline/contact number as an easy means of communication for communities to report accidents which requires rescue; (iii) establish Emergency Response/Rescue Team and Volunteer Groups and ensure that they are equipped with basic skills in various rescue operations; and (iv) maintain stockpile of unperishable goods for ready distribution in case of calamities including the beddings.

(a) BPLO to (i) require the taxpayer to submit sworn statement on gross sales/receipts to be used in the initial computation of tax due; and (ii) require the submission of copy of Certified Income Tax Returns as required in the Revised Revenue Code of the Municipality; and

(a) submit to the Sangguniang Bayan the proposals to revise the Market Code, which should contain all the necessary provisions on the market operation; and 

(b) instruct the Market Committee to (i) strictly manage the public market operation particularly on the execution and implementation of contracts; and (ii) monitor and intensify collections of goodwill/rights fees and market stall rentals.
